author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Melon Salad With Nectarines, Tomatoes and Basil  Recipe
commentBody: I saw this inspiring photo, read the blurb (love these people's approach), scanned the ingredients, and got right to work on my lunch.  I had apricots and Queen Anne cherries, neither very interesting eaten out of hand; small golden tomatoes; red onion; some leftover lemon vinaigrette; a hunk of asiago; and salted pistachios.  Together they made a delicious and healthy feast of my lunch, and I felt like I'd done something.  Thanks for this recipe and for loads of other great ones, NYT.

recipeTitle: Melon Salad With Nectarines, Tomatoes and Basil  Recipe
commentBody:
For once I found a recipe that looks as attractive when made at home as shown in the charming NYT Cooking image!
A few thoughts:
Vinegar not needed due to acidity of the lemon.
Mint is also a nice addition with the basil.
Why cut capers in half? No need!
Yellow watermelon, now availble at Trader Joe's, is a nice addition, replacing the nectarine.
Passed on the onion.
Made it exactly as written and without the layering of oil/lemon strategy. Both are great.

commentBody: For a more authentic Tinola, some refinements are in order. Certain steps and proportions here are little excessive. author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Seco de Pollo (Ecuadorian-Style Chicken Stew) Recipe
commentBody: So happy NYT has an Ecuadorian recipe! I consulted this recipe, and then a mix of videos in Spanish--search "seco de pollo receta." The "refrito" (onion/bell pepper/tomato mix), not usually called "sofrito" in Ecuador, tends to be cooked in achiote oil before being pureed. Take the seeds out of the tomatoes before you chop! If you cut the vegetables finely enough, you wouldn't need to blend. Once I added the chicken back, I braised at 325 in the oven in a covered Le Creuset.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Seco de Pollo (Ecuadorian-Style Chicken Stew) Recipe
commentBody:
Case, find below:

Ingredients:
•	2 cups rice
•	2 TSP olive oil/butter
•	1 shallot, finely diced
•	2 garlic cloves, minced
•	1 TSP salt
•	¾ TSP turmeric
•	1/8 TSP (a pinch) ground cinnamon
•	1/8 TSP (a pinch) ground coriander

Instructions: 	
•	Heat oil/butter, add shallot, garlic and sauté until just beginning to brown, ~3-4 minutes
•	Add salt, turmeric, cinnamon, coriander and stir until combined
•	Add rice and water / broth and boil, covered, for ~20 minutes

commentBody: Watch out for grit on the dried porcini, it'll ruin your meal. You can give them a gentle brush after the soak, maybe even a gentle rinse but obviously don't lose any of that flavor. Also I always drain the soaking water through a kitchen towel before putting it into the sauce - or carefully decant it to leave behind the dregs. And I'd use all the soaking water and reduce it down because that's where a lot of the flavor is.

commentBody: This was a big hit for a small dinner party. Dried porcini mushrooms are a bit pricey, but for an occasional meal, definitely worth it. I subbed in 3/4 cup white wine for half of the pasta water and it added an extra layer of flavor, to an already amazing recipe. I used two .7 ounce mushroom packets and was worried there wouldn’t be enough mushroom, but it was more than enough. I could have still added pasta for two more and been satisfied with the portion and flavors.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: I always make a recipe exactly as written before commenting. The only thing that I did differently was to use 50 grams of dried mushrooms instead of 40 grams as called for. This recipe was very disappointing. There wasn’t enough sauce for the pasta. The next day I sautéed shallots in butter and olive oil. Then I added 8 oz of sliced baby bella mushrooms and dried thyme and salt. After the moisture was rendered from the mushrooms I added this to the pasta with mushrooms from the night before. I added a splash of cognac (not necessary) and heated this and added parmesian cheese and a bit  of cream, maybe a quarter of a cup. I stirred the mixture while heating and obtained a lively velvety sauce. This was much better than the original recipe.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: I never comment. But I furtively made this recipe, which looked incredible, and wanted my picky children to eat it. So I offered them a choice: the usual Marcella Hazen tomato sauce with penne, or this. I made both, put them side by side, and put one piece of each into a bowl for my kids to decide for themselves which to eat for dinner. I just called it ‘Mom Pasta.’ BOTH kids asked for Mom Pasta, and my eight year old son ate three bowls! I still haven’t told them they ate a zillion mushrooms…for the first time. (The idea of mushrooms of course disgusts them), I love tricking my children into eating great food….

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: I live in a rural area and had planned to get dried porcinis during my latest trip “to the city” - but there were none to be found! I was set on making the recipe, so got 8 oz of cremini instead and, after removing stems, was left with about 6 oz mushrooms. Chopped extremely finely and cooked for more like 8-10 min vs the 3 as written for rehydrated porcini. Omitted the addition of soaking liquid (only added the 1.5 c pasta water). Turned out beautifully! I’d still like to try recipe as written but wanted to share an alternative take in case helpful for others with limited grocery offerings.

commentBody: I agree with the commenter who said that the red pepper is not Tuscan. It seems like EVERY recipe I encounter these days has red pepper flakes as if every dish needs heat. And the amount of black pepper in this dish is quite enough heat!

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
That this dish is insanely, five-star delicious (especially when tossed with mushroom fettuccine from a local specialty shop) does not mitigate some issues with its preparation. To wit:
•as other cooks have noted, you need to start the sauce before your pasta begins cooking. And you should use the full 1+ cup of porcini soaking liquid (minus the grit at bottom) and just a splash of pasta water at the end— not the other way around.
•no Italian cook I know tosses grated Parmesan into a dish while it’s still cooking. You add it at the end, otherwise half of it congeals and settles at the bottom of the pan (and later in the back side of my kitchen sponge).
•chopping up the porcini so finely seems a bit unnecessary as does 50 (50!) twists of the pepper mill. Yikes!! I came close, and it was delicious, but still …

commentBody: Both my husband and I were rather disappointed with this dish. I was skeptical about using only tomato paste rather than whole or crushed tomatoes & the need for heavy cream. But I went with the recipe based on the rave reviews. I did add some extra sautéed mushrooms per some comments. In the end, I don’t think it was worth the time or expense. I did not skimp on ingredients: I used good quality porcini, imported Italian tomato paste & tagliatelle, as well as parmigiano reggiano cheese. In the end, it was just okay. Sorry, but I would not make this again.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: This brilliant dish is much improved w/ caramelized shallots, dry marsala to deglaze pan. I use Costco dried mushroom, which are notoriously gritty & a little tough. I strain them through a coffee filter, rinse them lightly, then dice in a small food processor into a ground beef size. After cooking mushrooms w/ onions, add 2 tbsp butter & full cup of mushroom liquid. Simmer on low for ~30 minutes. Add pecorino Romano, parmesan & parsley. Then 1 cup pasta water. So much richer & more flavorful.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
This was amazing.  I made a gluten/dairy free version using dairy free parmesan and heavy cream made by emulsifying (with a milk frother!) oat milk, corn starch and a drop of olive oil.  The fake cream was so good that I also used it in the gluten/diary full version I made.  I am definitely making this again and will use anchovies.

Also, I saved all the remaining porcini water to use for heating up leftover pasta and for flavoring in other dishes.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
When I first saw this recipe I knew I needed to make it. Porcini mushrooms are an umami delight that I wish we could get here in the U.S., but the dried version gives us a reasonable facsimile. 

I added a pound of very good 80/20 ground beef to this, which I browned, with some salt and just a bit of Italian herb blend, at the beginning, drained off the fat (saving it) and put the meat aside in a bowl. I added it to the porcini mixture after it was cooked. The pasta water and porcini water did a good job of deglazing the pan to bring all of the fond from the ground beef and porcini mixture into the sauce. There was more porcini water than was needed for the sauce, so I tossed this into the pasta water to add a bit more flavor to the pasta. 

This dish was WONDERFUL!  My wife exclaimed that it was one of the best pasta dishes she’d ever had in our home.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
Amazing as-is! But, it was $20 just for the amount of dried porcini in the recipe. So, a bit pricey, although the rest of the recipe is so simple that I’d normally have everything on hand anyway, so rather inexpensive in hindsight!

Anyway, I’ve never had porcini before so I’m not sure if I could pick out the flavor profile. I think I’ll try it with fresh oyster and shiitake next time to see if I can spot the difference!

My husband and I polished off the whole pan. We served it with crusty sourdough that was lightly toasted and it was fantastic.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Used this recipe as inspiration. Soaked dried European porcini for much longer. Cooked Cipriani tagliarelle alongside. Skipped the pasta cooking water (used ALL the porcini soaking water instead). No cream. No cheese added in during cooking. Pan never gets "set aside"; pasta transferred "wet" from cooking pot to sauté pan, tossed & simmered in.  After plating, chopped reserved parsley tops & grated Piave Vecchio cheese strewn generously over the dark-colored stuff. Toss & eat. Yum.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
I changed this up a bit and did half the porcini but added 1lb ground beef. Doubled everything else. It turned out great. A few tips:

-Soak the porcini in a smaller amount of water so you can add it all to the sauce. No sense in discarding flavor. Add all the porcini water to the sauce and allow it to reduce for at least 30min covered. 

-Add 1 medium carrot and 1 large celery stock, minced, to stir fry with the mushrooms. This will give the sauce a more conventional Italian ragout flavor. 

-If the sauce is getting too think while reducing, add some chicken stock. 

Excellent recipe, will make again.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Tripled the recipe; vegan this time. Soaked 120 g porcini in 4 cups of cold water for about 6 hours; gently buzzed the squeezed shrooms in my mini food processor. Kept all the soaking water. Started with a sauteed shallot. No hot pepper flakes anywhere. Cooked 1 lb. of fettuccine. Saved about 4 cups of pasta water, but in the end only used almost all of the porcini water & no pasta water. Gently mixed several HEAPING tbsps of nutritional yeast at the end. No cream. CRAZY INTENSE flavor! YUM!

commentBody: This really is fantastic. I mostly stuck to the recipe, except I sautéd separately a small shallot (adds an underlying layer of sweetness in the end), added 3 anchovy fillets (and salted to taste), used a little more mushroom liquid vs pasta water and subbed half/half. I just finished dinner and am already dreaming of leftovers!

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: It always takes me aback, when I read something like, "red pepper flakes, are not Tuscan." The author spent her childhood in Tuscany--she's an authority.  Black pepper is more definitively Tuscan for the region around Florence, but even there, cooks use peperoncini to support flavors. There is a peperoncino conserva from that region, that's delicious. At the seaside of Tuscany, you'll find them in loads of dishes featuring seafood.

- used three GENEROUS tablespoons of cream
- added ground beef at the reheat phase for protein

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: I made this and it was delicious, even though I changed it quite a lot. I had 3 big field mushrooms I wanted to use up (are these a thing in the US? i think you could use any fresh mushroom and it would work but I guess an equivalent would be portabella) I chopped them very small and otherwise followed the recipe except I added a glug of red wine at the first reduction stage. I had less mushrooms than the recipe says but I forgot and used the same amount of water, so it took ages to reduce but when it did it was really worth it. I also did not add any parmasan to the sauce, I just sprinkled some on top (way less that stated- I'm watching my cholesterol) , it was still amazing and felt quite decadent for a weeknight! will make again for sure

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
I have been a NYT Cooking Comment lurker for years.  This was so good I must comment.  I havent worked with dried Porcini mushrooms before-  my husband walked through the kitchen and asked, are you making something with prosciutto? They are deliciously meaty.
I used fresh pasta from our local pasta shop.  We had just over a pound-  doubled the sauce as recommended - added 2 thinly sliced shallots to soften before adding the garlic and added the anchovies to the mushrooms which someone here recommended.  I dreamt of that pasta last night!  paired with a Kale Salad, creamed spinach, and a 19 year old Oregon Pinot Noir and had a very memorable evening.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Avocado, Edamame and Yuzu Dip With Furikake Recipe
commentBody: This sounded really good. But it has too many hard to find, otherwise not used ingredients for me to ever make it. Also, the article mentioned that the chef used to eat avocados with sugar. That suggested the large avocados rather than Haas avocados. Yet this recipe doesn’t say which kind to use. I’m thinking it meant haas bc it said 4 avocados. If I ever did come across Yuzus or the rice spice in one of my ethnic groceries here in South Florida I would try to make this with the large sweet avocados. My caribbean and south american friends have explained to me that those are used for sweets while the haas are used for savories.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: This is Lisa Kyung Gross--founder of the League of Kitchens and author of this cookbook.  Some people have said that this is too much salt.  The salt is RINSED OFF before serving.  You salt the cabbage with 3 Tablespoons of Diamond Crystal Kosher salt (the large grains of salt are important--table salt or fine sea salt will make this much too salty), crunch it in with your hands, and let it sit for 10 minutes. Then you wash off the salt before dressing it.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Also, just a note that "flathead Taiwanese cabbage" is not Napa cabbage.  It looks like someone has taken a conventional cabbage and flattened it.  It's softer and more mild than conventional green cabbage, but is closer to that flavor than Napa.  You can make this with Napa, but it has a different flavor and texture. If you use conventional cabbage, you need to salt it for closer to 15 minutes so that the salt can penetrate the tougher leaf (then make sure to rinse off all the salt).

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
Looks like some people are trying to avoid garlic. 
When I cook for people who find garlic too sharp, I microwave the sliced garlic in a small microwave bowl with oil for a very short time. 
I can use just the oil or the oil with the garlic, depending on the person’s needs. And we still get to have that marvelous flavor.
Some folks who have trouble with garlic do fine with the taste of garlic in oil. They just need to avoid actual pieces of garlic.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Kosher salt (like Morton's) and Diamond Crystal salt are not the same and are not interchangeable and shouldn't be swapped without adjustments. Three tablespoons of Morton's is denser (heavier, saltier) than 3 tablespoons of Diamond Crystal. In some recipes, it matters. For a more in-depth explanation of these salts and their history: https://www.seriouseats.com/ask-the-food-lab-do-i-need-to-use-kosher-salt https://www.nytimes.com/2023/08/01/dining/diamond-crystal-kosher-salt.html

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
In the UK, a tender cabbage similar to Napa (Chinese) cabbage or Taiwanese cabbage commonly available in the USA is Hispi cabbage, also known as pointed cabbage or sweetheart cabbage. It has a conical shape, tender leaves, and a mild, sweet flavor, making it comparable to the texture and taste of Napa cabbage.

If Hispi cabbage isn’t available, spring greens (a loose-leaf, early-season cabbage) or a young savoy cabbage can also serve as a substitute for Napa cabbage in many recipes. These options are tender, versatile, and work well in stir-fries, salads, or as a steamed side dish.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: In my experience I have found that Napa Cabbage is far tastier than regular cabbage, easier to cook and to consume. Be it raw in a salad, in a simple stir fry and in a soup. Sometimes the actual taste can be a little sweet which makes the thicker white ribs perfect for soups. I slice the ribs and add first into soup to allow the flavour to be well absorbed, and lastly add sliced leaves.  Napa Cabbage works well in any salad, and can blanched in boiling water and then dressed with oyster sauce.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
I used 1.5 T Morton’s kosher salt, 1 garlic clove, and 2 T avocado oil. I liked it a lot and I will make it again! 

Now that I know what to expect, with one successful attempt under my belt, I can add on a bit more next time. 

I added only one garlic clove because I read other comments about the garlic being too much. 

I also do not have Red Diamond kosher salt. A very helpful comment included the link to an article about the differences in salts by Kenji Alt-Lopez which led me to my estimate of halving the salt. 

I used avocado oil because I couldn’t find my toasted sesame oil until after I finished tossing the salad. I added a bit.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: At first, I thought there's nothing special about it, but I couldn't stop eating it. Napa or Taiwanese cabbage would be ideal, but I used red cabbage and it worked fine. Please don't skip soaking it in salt water - it crisps up the cabbage, brings out the sweetness and prevents water leaking. 3tbsp of big salt crystals seem about right for me. After letting sit in the fridge for half a day, I saw barely any water pooling and no drop in quality. This is such a simple dish so prep work is key.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: I wasn't as wowed as I'd hoped to be, alas. I used a tender cabbage from my garden, more than a pound, but found the salad too salty with 3T of kosher salt.  (Yes, I did rinse it, and yes I did use Diamond Crystal.) A sprinkle of sugar and splash of rice vinegar made it more pleasing as a side to sheet-pan Japanese BBQ chicken thighs...but not quite addictive. A few days' fermentation of the salted cabbage (before rinsing & dressing) would likely boost the umami.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
I could tell this would be addictive with one change: less salt or more rinsing. I used the 3 tbl of salt for half of a very big napa cabbage (how many cups of chopped cabbage would be helpful), rinsed it three times over, and it was pretty much inedible due to saltiness (and I love salt). I would use 2 tbl next time and rinse it 5 times.....
Also note that the amount of cabbage will significantly reduce in volume once salted and rinsed. I started with 2.5 c. and ended up with .75 c.!

commentBody: I agree with a couple of the recent comments about saltiness and need for acid. I made it as written, with a smallish half head of Napa. The dressing could do without added salt, and would benefit from some acid, maybe vinegar or lime juice (which seemed likely from just reading the recipe).

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
Well, I made this dish with high hopes.  After a lot of washing/kneading cabbage, salt content was still too high.  Dressing alone wasn’t enough to tamp it down.  And finally, dressing was a little … “flat”.

Not sure where/how i went wrong, but will give this another try.  Too many positive reviews tells me I messed up somewhere.  Would LOVE suggestions or thoughts from others!

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
After reading all the comments, I felt equipped to try this recipe. I weighed a pound of Napa cabbage, I used half as much salt by volume (because I was using Morton's kosher), and I rinsed thoroughly before dressing. However, it was still far too salty.

I think the main issue could be that I "crunched and scrunched" too vigorously. I did this step as if I was making sauerkraut (so there was cabbage juice & brine at the bottom of the bowl when I was done), which probably made it nearly impossible to rinse thoroughly enough. Perhaps a gentler massage is what the author has in mind?

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Oh, yes! Taiwanese flathead cabbage is of the highest quality.  At our farmers market in Hollywood, CA , there’s one vendor who sells this.  It’s a bit pricey, but worth it, and his is also organic! Some people are a bit surprised to hear that this variety is brilliant (raw) in salads: tender and slightly sweet. I don’t do the pre-salting, and it’s just fine!  Adding a bit of grated ginger and Mirin to the dressing is fabulous.  No Mirin?  A dash of Japanese Rice Vinegar will suffice.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: This reminds me of a recipe (based on ordinary cabbage) I used to cook after remodeling my house during the day and working the night shift at my job. (I used to have limitless energy. Now that I'm old, not so much.) I'd peel 3 or 4 potatoes, cover with water and a tbsp of salt in a deep pot, with some cloves of fresh garlic. While that came to a boil, I'd cut a head of cabbage into pieces and dump them over the potatoes. Cover and simmer till all is tender. Leftovers for lunch.

commentBody: I made this today under less than ideal circumstances.  No Diamond Crystal Salt, no sesame seeds, etc.  I used Kirkland pink salt, and after rinsing and spinning it was not too salty.  I have my Amazon order in for the right salt for next time.  I wouldn't refer to what I made as addictive, but after a few shots of tamari and white vinegar, it was pretty good.  I know the D Crystal Salt is a big deal, but I sure hope it doesn't get to where all interesting recipes require it.

commentBody:

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Although there’s no hard and fast rule, any recipe titled Yamitsuki suggests fast and easy with one or maybe two main ingredients. So, you can get fancy by adding chicken , carrots etc, but that wouldn’t be called Yamitsuki. Also, it shouldn’t take so many steps. Akin to American “dump” recipes, you simply add all ingredients at once, which means in this case, you use less salt and don’t rinse it off. It may require more massaging but keeps the flavor.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Ipo Pain Perdu (Coconut-Bread French Toast) Recipe
commentBody:
So. I have made this three times now. 
Holy smokes is this yummy. Yes it’s a BIT denser than other French toast breads. But to its favor! 
I recommend using dry active and per usual doing an activation with 1/4 warm water. So reduce your coconut milk by 1/4c. I also did a proof/rise after mixing and forming into a ball for about 1hr. Then after placing in loaf pan doing a second proof/rise for 30mins to an hour. Bake 45 at 350. Bake with a loose aluminum foil cover too. Helps it not brown too much on top. 
This bread is fine alone but really benefits as a French toast. The crumb is wonderfully thicker but not at all “grainy”. The idea that it has a different texture loans from the coconut pieces. If that’s not your cup of tea then maybe use a finer coconut flake? 
Also after doing some research there is much more coconut than suggested. So I doubled it.  Yum!

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Mashed ‘Ulu With Passion Fruit Chutney Recipe
commentBody:
On Kauai we have multiple places to get the Ulu for free. I have been using it instead of tofu. We boil the fresh Ulu whole in the skin...it is so much easier than trying to peel the fresh, uncooked fruit.

Once peeled and cored, I mash the Ulu, press it into a half pan and freeze it, then cut the 1/2" thick frozen product into squares, tightly wrap, then freeze. It is so healthy. 

I made this recipe following the instructions to the letter. It was delicious.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
The scones in the photo seem rather thick.  I'd try slicing the scones in half horizontally giving 12 sandwiches, in lieu of the 6 of the recipe. Otherwise, the ratio of filling to pastry seems way out of whack, similar to how the Welsh/Brits used to make their sandwiches.  

Two pieces of thinly sliced bread, no more than 3"x3" after the crusts were removed. Once assembled, the sandwiches were cut into 4 tiny triangles. The ham sandwich contained one paper-thin slice of ham and nothing else.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: My favorite hack when a recipe calls for lining a pan with parchment paper is to thoroughly wet the parchment paper, wring it out well, give it a vigorous shake and then line the pan with the now malleable damp paper. author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: It seems folks are asking about crystallized ginger to sprinkle on top of the icing. These are usually sold as slices or chunks cured in sugar and sold as a dry good (rather than in a liquid syrup). Some are more like candied ginger where as others are more like dried crystallized ginger (similar to the method used for dried apricots and other fruit).

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: If you want wonderfully spicy candied ginger, then save your small diced ginger after you use it here. It is so easy to crystallize, and half the crystalizing process was done by doing the early cook of the cubes for this recipe. There are simple recipes around, but I added the ginger cubes to pan, added equal weight baker's sugar, and two TBSP water then cooked for 20 mins on med high/stirring and cooled on a sugar coated parchment paper pan. Easy and great spicy cube of ginger amazing...!

commentBody:

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: I’m a big fan of ginger and I’m on a life long quest looking for the best Gingersnap recipe out there that has plenty of snap and ginger flavor. This cake satisfies my needs.  The ginger flavor doesn’t come at you like a sledge hammer but by the time I reached the end of my first piece my mouth was on fire.  This moist ginger honey tea cake is fantastically and deliciously satisfying.  For the others who saw failure try calibrating your over oven, get some fresh ginger and try it again.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: I baked this delicious cake yesterday. Already half gone. Some reviews mention that theirs didn't come out with a strong ginger taste. Mine is even spicy. My trick? Before I even turned on the oven I sliced the unpeeled ginger ( no time for peeling & chopping) and put it in the pan with the water AND the 1/2 cup of honey. The ginger steeped before and after in this honey water salabat. Then I followed the recipe (BTW Thnx for the wet parchment paper trick, perfection) after I strained the ginger I froze the honey water steeped chunks for tea. It came out super delish. Will repeat.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Made per instructions, but without icing. The only change I would make is adding another warming spice—maybe cardamom, for some mystery or cinnamon, for the comfort. Very enjoyable, and not super sweet. Will make again.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Made this cake as instructed but instead of chopping the ginger, I finely grated it using a microplane grater which I think brought out more of the ginger flavour.  The lemon didn’t contribute much.  Like others have suggested, another warming spice is needed.  Perhaps cardamom?  And perhaps lime instead of lemon? I might make it again with these alternatives and I’ll let you know if I do. But otherwise, I don’t think I need to make this cake again. It’s “ok” but not amazing and there’s just so many other recipes out there to try.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
Don't know what I did wrong, but wasn't nearly as gingery as I wanted—perhaps it was the old ground ginger that I had? But I also oversteeped the ginger root accidentally, and I would've thought that would have made it more gingery, and not less. As a cake though, it was quite good. Definitely going for fresher ground ginger, and upping the amount of diced ginger I use in the tea. 

Other commentors suggestion to candy the ginger after you steep it is good!

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Lazi Ji (Chongqing Chicken With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody:
Fuchsia Dunlop is the "Julia Child" of authentic Sichuan cooking!

Anthony Bourdain, on the infamous heat level of Sichuan’s food: "It’s the spicy, sensualist heartland of all the things I love about China… food that can burn you down to a charred, smoking little stump."
Bourdain, on Lazi Ji, a Sichuan spicy chicken specialty: "The dish, in practical terms, is a game of finding the bits of chicken in the mountain of butt-burning goodness. C’mon, it’s fun for the whole family."

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Lazi Ji (Chongqing Chicken With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody:
Made this as directed.  Very tasty.  Reminds me of Chongqing Chicken I had in China.

Not sure it's worth the effort to bone skin-on thighs and then cut 3/4" chunks.  Much of the skin was lost when cubing the chicken.  As for prep time it doesn't include boning the chicken and the 5 min allotted can't include cutting and seeding 2 C of chiles.  I spent over an hour on the chicken and chiles.  I'm not sure how this feeds four.  Two of us polished it off with rice.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: No gelatin in Mehallabiya! Its an authentic arabian pudding. In Kuwait, they flavor it with orange blossom water. In Egypt, with vanilla or rose water. In Lebanon, rose water and sometimes mastic. Every Arab country have their own version of Mehallabiya. Kuwaitis call it “Emhalbiya”, Egyptians “Mehallabiya”, in Lebanon and Syria “Mhalbiyyee”. If you have access to buffalo milk, use it here! It takes it to another level. If you have some clotted cream; mix a TS when u turn off the heat.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Those who ask about other uses for Mastic, let me tell you about some delicious ways of incorporating it into ur dishes, the arabs way! 1) before cooking ur rice, melt 3 to 5 mastic pearls with some oil and butter or ghee, then add ur rice and liquid, the result will be heavenly! 2) add a few pearls to ur chicken/meat stock pot and thank me later! 3) melt a pearl or two with some fats before searing ur meats. (Dont be generous with mastic, it’ll impart a sour taste if used more than needed)

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Mehalabi was a favorite street-cart treat in Limassol as an eleven-year old visiting my father’s birthplace of Cyprus for the first time.   Families in the villages each had their own way of preparing it—with milk or water, or with or without nuts. Sometimes cut into cubes, or sometimes as a firmer pudding spooned out of a small individual cup portion. The new and unique taste of rose water splashed over a cool and refreshing mixture was lovely!

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
I was skeptical that a tree resin grown only on the southern coast of a single island in the Aegean could be an affordable mass-market ingredient (still dubious that it's terribly sustainable, really), so I ran the numbers:

The most popular source for mastic tears on Amazon right now is 20g for $15, or $0.75/g.  Powdered ingredients seem to clock in at around 4g/tsp, so one recipe's worth of mastic is going to cost about $3. Make of that what you will.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
A disaster. The pudding never jelled, but the gummy mastic instantly clung to the inside of my kettle as soon as I turned up the heat, and stirring only splashed it onto my cooktop, where it congealed and has been very difficult to remove. Even after cooling, the pudding never thickened. I just had to throw it all out. Or POUR it all out, actually, down the drain of my kitchen sink. 

It took me more time to clean out my pots and clean up my kitchen than it did too make the recipe.

NEVER AGAIN!

commentBody: These jackfruit tacos were great and very easy to make. I used organic canned jackfruit (sold at Whole Foods). I used half of the instructed oil and the dish was excellent. author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Ricotta Toast With Roasted Grapes Recipe
commentBody:
I too am "allergic" to Balsamic vinegar; indeed all vinegar except ACV. 

It's called Mast Cell Activation Syndrome (MCAS). Also known as Histamine Intolerance. Has to do with the fermentation vs the grapes themselves. Fresh grapes are fine; but not fermented, processed or aged *anything*. It's a boring diet, requires lotsa subs.
 
Here I'd use ACV, fresh grapes; Sourdough vs usual yeasted bread, tho not all can tolerate it. Lemons are N/A, too. Sigh

It's v hard. Have patience with us, please!

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Thai Tea Tres Leches Cake Recipe
commentBody:
What a brilliant idea.  I've always liked the concept of tres leches cakes, but in practice they're invariably so supersweet that I find them cloying.  Cutting all that sweetness with the astringency of tea seems like a tremendous idea.

But before investing in the Thai powder, I think I'll experiment by heating the milk and steeping black tea in it.  This might result in an unwelcome color, but I'll risk it.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Thai Tea Tres Leches Cake Recipe
commentBody: I made this for my husband’s birthday (he’s a huge fan of all thing tres leches). This cake blew his socks off! I used ChaTraMue brand tea; it has a little sugar, but I cut the added sugar by 1/3 for baked good recipes, so it didn’t much matter. This cake is a fair amount of process, but worth it. Since it was my first go at making it, aside from cutting sugar, I limited deviation. To the dry mix, added a pinch of cinnamon, cardamom, mace, 1/2 tsp vanilla bean, and 1/2tsp salt to punch it up.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Thai Tea Tres Leches Cake Recipe
commentBody: I tried making this recipe's cake from scratch but found it challenging, time-consuming, and not that good. Instead, I started using yellow/white box cake. The box recipe usually calls for water, but substitute it with whole milk. I also whisk 1/2-1 tsp of cardamom powder into the box cake dry mix. I follow NYT's recipe for the Thai tea milk mix, and I soak the box cake with it. I've made this cake 2-3 times this way, and it's always gone in minutes. People are always shocked its box cake.

recipeTitle: Thai Tea Tres Leches Cake Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly as the recipe states (with an 3-day soaking time) and it was mind-bogglingly good. Lighter than I expected, too! And of course, the lime zest and coconut flakes are a must, both for flavor and texture.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Thai Tea Tres Leches Cake Recipe
commentBody:
It is against my religion to soak cakes in anything that changes the texture,😱😁 My daughter wanted me to make this. I love Thai ice tea, one I indulge in very rarely. As a compromise, I made the Thai Tres leches about 2 cups and used a slice or two of regular cake to indulge her . Leftover tea diluted with water and ice made me an indulgent Thai ice tea. 
If anyone else feels the same way about soaking a big batch of lovely cake, here is another option.

recipeTitle: Thai Tea Tres Leches Cake Recipe
commentBody: This was really good and so fun for the White Lotus finale :) The cake did have to bake a little longer, like 10 minutes, and it was still a little underdone in the middle (next time I would wait until there was NO jiggle even if it gets a little brown) but still delish. I couldn’t find Thai tea leaves so used instant powder and decreased the condensed milk by half a cup because the powder is sweet and it worked great. Just kept adding packets until it tasted like tea (probably drank a good amount of milk tasting it lol) - I think it was about 3/4ths cup powder. I agree the lime zest and coconut is ESSENTIAL - do not skip the lime, this is very sweet. I would even want to maybe find a way to either make the cake or the milk mixture a little lime-y - will try some zest in the cake next time!

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Thai Tea Tres Leches Cake Recipe
commentBody:
This ruled! Made it for a dinner party and it was a huge hit. Kind of obsessed with the sponge recipe—SO light and gorgeaous. 

I got Pantai tea mix without realizing it isn’t a powder, so I added ~70g to the tres leches in order get the strength I wanted. For the flip: I cut mine as 5x3, so I ended up taking out one row and setting it on a cutting board. I flipped the remaining slices one at a time, then flipped and replaced the remaining slices and scraped any remaining liquid back over the cake.  Last note: the measurements vs. can amounts IS perverse, but now I have everything I need for Thai iced tea. 10/10!

Would totally make this again.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Miyar Taushe (Lamb and Squash Stew) Recipe
commentBody:
1. The recipe does say later to use only half the squash. I used the whole squash (a large one) and it was great
2.  I put all ingredients in a slow cooker with the squash quartered and laid on top, open side down. After4 hours I removed the squash, took seeds out and rind off. Put lamb with most of the onions in bowl and returned squash to slow cooker and mashed it with the immersion blender. Returned lamb and onions and it was great!!! and oh so easy

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Miyar Taushe (Lamb and Squash Stew) Recipe
commentBody:
1. I added half a farmer’s market scotch bonnet and fished it out before blending. Plenty spicy.
2. I also used the whole squash. I added 1/4 tsp of regular nutmeg. 
3. Needed quite a bit more salt than called for. Layered it in.
4. I also just threw in the spinach at the very end.
5. Served over rice and topped with some diced tomato in addition to seeds to give it some freshness/acidity.

I wasn’t 100% pleased with the texture of the lamb shank. A bit tough. Maybe more time braising?

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Miyar Taushe (Lamb and Squash Stew) Recipe
commentBody:
Browning the meat would develop more flavor. 

I wouldn’t bother with the expense (and mild inconvenience) of lamb neck next time.

I was impatient and couldn’t wait for calabash nutmeg. Freshly grated nutmeg, coriander, and cardamom (all toasted, 1:1:1 ratio) was a pleasant substitute.

I think you could just throw all of the veg (squash, tomato, onion, peppers) onto a large sheet pan and roast it all at once. Cuts out a step later on, and let’s you relax more on your stew-day.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Chickpeas Escabeche With Plantain Strips Recipe
commentBody: We made this as an amuse-bouche for a dear friend's 60th birthday dinner party. While we didn't change the recipe for it or the homemade sofrito (they were perfect), we did add some fancy and highly recommended flourishes: replaced canned chickpeas with 1/2 lb dried Rancho Gordo Ceci Piccolo (small chickpeas) simmered up with aromatics, and added seeds from one pomegranate for color, sweetness and crunch. It was next-level. We gave it an "amuse"-ing highbrow-lowbrow name:  "Chickpea Caviar."

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: One of my favorite salads for years and I always order it when I find it in a resto. However, a few years ago I dropped the breading and frying of the chevre. Instead, I simply heat part of a log of goat cheese in the oven or microwave with olive oil and fines herbs. Then I plunk the warm soft chevre on my dressed greens and eat with crusty bread. For me, it's the same or even better and reminds me of my years living in Paris.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Green Salad With Warm Goat Cheese (Salade de Chèvre Chaud) Recipe
commentBody: I love this story. It reminds me of when I was young and traveling on my own, always with low funds. I have made this recipe for many years, and guests are always amazed at how delicious it is, yet so simple. I've never used the egg mixture, but it makes sense...and after patting them with the bread crumbs, I put the little cheese rounds in a toaster oven...my go-to kitchen appliance. And thanks for the idea of sherry vinegar instead of red wine vinegar, which I don't use much any more.

commentBody: This is one of my favorite salads- but the first I have heard of frying breaded goat cheese.  I will try it out.  The more standard preparation is to smear the goat cheese on small pieces of toasted soft bread (not baguette).  There are other variations.  One I enjoyed in Paris had the goat cheese on toast pieces with pesto and honey.  Also, there is no rule that the salad has to be only greens.  Add what you like.

commentBody:
This is one of my favorite salads—I taught myself how to make it after first having it in NYC during college! There’s a version @ local haunt here in LA that bulks it up with wild rice—have made that version too, and even cut back on the cheese (a little) & added grilled tofu (I know, eye roll), but these are excellent ways to bulk it up if you’re skipping the bread. 

That being said, I’m going to get back to basics and make this exactly as written as fast as you can say “Oui!”

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: I make hummus without tahini. Into the food processor: rinsed canned chick peas, olive oil, fresh lemon juice, garlic cloves to taste, salt to taste. I add bits of water to loosen it up or add a roasted red pepper. For heat, sometimes I add a pinch of cayenne. To serve, I will spread on a plate, top with minced tomatoes, cucumbers, red peppers, chopped roasted pistachios, minced parsley and mint, then drizzle with olive oil. I think hummus takes a fair amount of salt to be tasty.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
Didn't have hazelnuts so I used roasted, salted almonds. Eliminated roasting and removing skins, though hazelnuts would add character.  I tripled the lemon juice and sumac; doubled the rice vinegar, and used more salt to punch up the flavor. 
Use GROUND coriander! Since the recipe didn't specify, I used whole seeds. Bad mistake. They didn't break down in the food processor. Nobody wants to chew and swallow the hard, dry pieces. So it's like eating watermelon and spitting out the seeds.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
Years ago, when my brief fling with a Greek Revival house in Walpole, New Hampshire had come to an end, I went over to a neighbor's garden. The owner had the same name as a tragic heroine in a Brian Moore novel; she, too, was private and reticent. I said I had a stash of old, well-rotted hummus I wanted to give her; bemused, she followed me to my compost pile and gently corrected me on the pronunciation of humus. 
If she's still alive, I hope she enjoys this ancient recipe as much as I did.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
I find hummus forgiving and flexible. This recipe, the article, and the comments sort of point that out: Use nuts and seasoning ratios that work for you. 

I actually like making chunkier hummus than the totally smooth one from the store. That’s part of what makes it homemade for me. Also, I use the entire two cans of the beans and just scale up the other ingredients to taste so that I don’t have to deal with the leftover beans.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: This recipe taught me a lesson I should've known: if something doesn't sound right to you, don't say, "How could the NYT be wrong? I'll make it just as written even though I doubt something!" Instead, follow your instincts. My concern with this was that whole coriander seed would create a gritty, unpleasant texture. That's correct. (If I'd read the notes, I'd have seen someone else say so.) I'll make this again, but will pulverize the toasted caraway/coriander/sesame mix before using.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: This is a excellent as written. My husband doesn’t like raw garlic, and so many hummus recipes go overboard on the garlic, so was really happy to find a garlic-free hummus recipe. The warm spices shine through and the sumac brings a citrusy tartness. I used canned garbanzo beans and instead of water, used the liquid from the beans. My only caveat is this make a lot. I mean a lot!  Hoping it will freeze as we won’t get through all of it.  Next time, I’ll halve the recipe.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
This is similar to regular hummus, yet the spice profile is so unique. Really delicious. 

The only unroasted pistachios I could find we shelled, and I have no time for shelling nuts on a weeknight, so I subbed roasted and salted pistachios. It worked fine by just adding a little less salt.

If you have a blender, use it. It makes hummus so much better, even if it is a bit of a pain to scrape out the hummus. Using a Vitamix has transformed my relationship with home-made hummus (in a good way).

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
The best Hummus for my taste is the simplest, available as street food all over the Middle East. By the time hummus travelled to North America it morphed into something else. If you make it home with some personal changes, great.
However any hummus available in North American food markets is a joke. Little Tahini,lots of Vinegar and/or lemon juice. No taste of Hummus. I add Almond  butter and a few drops of roasted sesame oil to my hummus. Makes it taste like what you get in the Middle East.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
As noted, there's a difference between *white* or "poison" sumac, which most humans have violent allergic reactions to (and thus, it should never be consumed by anyone) and *red* sumac, which is an ingredient in many Middle Eastern dishes that adds a tangy, tart. almost citrusy note - and which is, for most but not all, quite safe. 

Still, a quick google search indicates that those with cashew or mango allergies should avoid red sumac, too.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Rhus coriaria is the name for Middle Eastern sumac. All sumacs are in the cashew family, and are related, but poison sumac is a distant cousin. The type we have here in the US, most typically staghorn sumac, is a relative. Indigenous people in the US have used staghorn sumac (and the related varieties of sumac native to these shores) for medicinal and food preparations since, well, ever. The popular “sumac lemonade” recipe is one such preparation. If harvesting, please do so ethically.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
Made exactly as listed, and appreciated the mellow, earthy notes.

Like many others mentioned, straining through a fine screen got out all the funky grit from the seed skins and unprocessed nut crumbs (the blender didn't get it all, even after a long run).

I feel that the outcome is now a 'base hummus' which I can dress up in different ways.

commentBody: This didn't win me over. Good hummus is simple and straightforward with just a few ingredients, although Americans tend to add way too much garlic. author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: For people who shy away from unnatural ingredients, try making strawberry soda from real strawberries and sugar and then adding sparkling water. To make the strawberries, hull and halve 1 1/2 pounds strawberries. Toss with about 3/4 cups of sugar in a metal bowl. Cover the bowl tightly with plastic wrap. Place over a bowl of simmering water and leave for 30 - 45 minutes. Remove from heat and when cool, strain the juice. Or puree the whole thing. Add sparkling water to taste.

commentBody:
This was a fun read, nostalgically recalling 
other summertime treats. I remember making, in high school, a dessert called ‘Frozen Passion’ - one large bottle of orange soda and one can of sweetened condensed milk, hand cranked in an old-fashioned ice cream freezer (the only kind available, in my day). Was this concoction overly sweet, artificially colored, nutritionally dubious? Of course. Was it also delicious, easy, fun, memorable? You betcha…..

commentBody: I made a non-dairy version. Used grape Jello, half a can of coconut whipping cream and a couple dollops of frozen grape juice concentrate. author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: This is a classic Texas dessert for potlucks, bunco/game nights, etc. It is not fancy, so while puff pastry may seem like a noble idea...just don't. For some reason the Pillsbury crescent dough just works, giving it more of that intended sopapilla texture (and I don't use canned dough otherwise!). A little citrus zest - orange is especially good - added to the cream cheese filling is a great addition, though.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: I did this for years for my staff. Simple and thrifty for about 50 people at a time.  I bought 12 -14 cans of crescent rolls - laid half out on big cookie sheets, pinched the roll seams together, spread the cream cheese mix whipped with well drained crushed pineapple, right to edges. It doesn't drip or leak. Top w/2d layer of rolls, seams pinched, then cover liberally with fine brown sugar mix -NO lumps.Bake. Cool. Barely trim baked edges & cut diagonally - @1" wide. Looks fancy piled high.

commentBody: I made this without the quilquiña and locoto as my usual sources (Amazon, etc) did not carry them. Providing a link to an online source for such unusual ingredients would help. Before adding the potato and peas, I tasted it for seasoning. It was a bit on the bland side so I added a bit more cumin and a half cup of aji panca, an ingredient I had recently bought for another recipe I found here at the Times. That did the trick and the end result was a nicely flavored soup that was worth the effort.

I'd recommend the Cilantro substitution, besides the fact Quilquiña/Papalo won't be easy to find for many - thank heavens. I've worked with thousands and thousands of ingredients and none made me spit it out onto the floor and literally lick my husband's shirt trying to remove the awfulness like this did. Horrid. To say it's stronger than Cilantro is an understatement.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Spaghettini With Bottarga and Colatura Recipe
commentBody: Had no time to buy bottarga & colatura (plus kinda pricey when cooking this for the first time), so I went with tarama (not taramasalata!) which is salted carp roe. Also made the yellow tomato sauce as described but with fresh heirloom tomatoes, and cooked them with some olive oil and s p for 10 mins. Sauce came out delicious & very easy. Loved the dish!

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Spaghettini With Bottarga and Colatura Recipe
commentBody: This sounded so intriguing -- such a beautifully written story -- and  happened to have a bar of bottarga (avgotaraho in Greek) plus a jar of the grated roe. Here in Athens Trikalinos bottarga or avgotaraho has been the gold standard of this delicacy for decades. You can find it in the US. José Andrés touts it and it has won international awards. I used oyster sauce, faute de mieux, olive oil, and chopped parsley. I still love bottarga sliced thin with a bit of lemon, but this was yummy.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Shrimp molt off their old shell as they grow.  Their new shell is right underneath the one they shed. It is very soft - like a soft shell crab.  It is hard as the dickens to peel cooked shrimp with new, soft shells.  But they are perfectly edible, except for the legs and tail.  Although I have seen people in Hawaii and other areas where shrimp are caught pop the entire thing, sans head, in their mouths.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
To devein a shrimp with the shell on, fold the shrimp in half around its legs to part the shell pieces along the backside, slip a toothpick crosswise about a quarter inch below the surface, between the shell pieces (perpendicular to the length of the shrimp) and pull straight up. The digestive tract will come out with it. 

That said, this recipe is bomb as hell and if you cook the shrimp right- dry em out and coat them, fry them hot enough- the shells really are just a bit of crunch.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
Wonderfully written recipe. I subbed in all-purpose flour because all of my rice flour was sacrificed in a butter mochi experiment. All-purpose gives it more a 'Popeyes'-texture fry instead of a crispy, light coating like you would get from the rice flour. I also removed the shells from my shrimp during prep because I just can't be bothered to peel as I eat. 
After growing up on the island of Oahu, I will say no recipe can beat what you get fresh off a shrimp truck. But this is a good start!

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
2 Tbsp Panko
1 Tbsp flour
Scant 1 tsp paprika
1 tsp coarse kosher salt

Two big spoonfuls of jar garlic — don’t cook too long in butter.

For shell-less shrimp, cook about 2 mins per side for first batch, then taper down with each successive batch. Last batch is prob 60-90 secs per side.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
The skillet temperature when you cook the shrimps can get very hot. The butter may overheat and the garlic may brown and become bitter when poured with the shrimps. Also, the excess flour/spices coating may burn and impart an unpleasant taste. Next time, I will use a separate skillet for the shrimps and the butter/garlic, and will plop the shrimps, once cooked, into the butter/garlic skillet to finalize the dish. 
Finally, the shells prevent the flour/spice coating from sticking to the shrimps.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Norinj Pilau (Rice With Candied Orange Peel, Saffron and Lamb) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! It's not a weekday dish as it takes most of the day unless you make the rice in advance. I used regular orange peels for the syrup and added some lemon juice to make it more tart. I loved the orange-syrup flavored rice that went on the top, but did not get that bright orange color shown in the image, except for the candied peels. Careful of the timing when boiling the rice - 7 min was too long for me and it was a bit mushy. Still very tasty. Don't skip the pistachios.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Norinj Pilau (Rice With Candied Orange Peel, Saffron and Lamb) Recipe
commentBody: I halved the recipe, and used a rice cooker to parcook the rice (turn it off when it goes from "cook" to "warm"). I had made candied orange peel from navel oranges a few weeks ago to garnish a rosca de reyes, so I just sliced that thinly and added cardamom, saffron and sugar and water for the orange syrup. Since the lamb pieces were larger and contained bones, I removed the meat from the bones after braising it and added it to the rice. So tasty!!! Would make again. Definitely comfort food...

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: The pan must be plain, not non-stick, and not greased, because the batter needs something to cling to as it rises. And re the order of mixing—baking powder begins to work when it gets wet, so you don’t want that sitting around very long. I would probably get the dry and wet ingredients mixed, then do merengue, then combine the wet and dry, then incorporate the merengue.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
Meringue will not deflate if egg whites are beat properly. 1) 68°F (20°C) egg whites; 2) on med low until just foamy, but translucent about 1 minute; 3) add cream of tartar, then beat on med low until volume increases and eggs are white and creamy; 4) GRADUALLY add sugar while beating—do not dump it in at once; 5) after sugar is added, increase mixer speed to med high and beat to desired peak level.

It has to do with stabilizing ionic bonds and preventing disulfide bonds.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: This cake resembles the lemon scented sponge cake my mother and grandmother made. They used a dozen eggs. My mother made a wine nut version. Five years before she passed at ninety-five, she showed me how to make my favorite passover version of this cake.  She mixed the yolks, sugar and wine, added dry ingredients and nuts, beat the egg whites with cream of tartar until stiff peaks formed and folded the yolk into the mixture and baked. Always a winner and in my opinion tastier than the lemon one.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Per a commenter's note below: potato FLOUR and potato STARCH are two entirely different products. Never substitute one for the other. Potato FLOUR is quite heavy and not the same lightening agent as potato STARCH, and will in fact have the opposite effect.  Potato FLOUR might be added to a recipe to add moisture, especially if baking gluten free, but only a very very small quantity. Potato STARCH, as the recipe states, is quite light and starchy,  tolighten baked gods and give stability.

commentBody: I made the cake exactly as the recipe called for. But I found it rather boring, so when I served it at a potluck dinner party I whipped up a simple, yet fabulously delicious sauce for it. I used a packet of frozen passionfruit purée from Goya (Available in many grocery store freezers in the Hispanic section). I cooked it with half a cup of sugar,  a Half cup of orange juice, And quarter cup of rum. I let it simmestirring,until it got syrup, about 20 minutes. Everybody loved the cake and preferred it with the sauce drizzled over each slice.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Delicious recipe. I didn't have a pan with a removable bottom so I lined the bottom with parchment and the cake rose properly and came out of the pan easily. I wanted to use gluten free flour so I created a self-rising version by making 2 cups of flour because it was easier mathwise (220 grams of flour and 32 grams of cornstarch, sifted twice and re-measured to have 1-3/4 cups needed for the recipe) and it came out perfectly.  Will definitely make again.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Not sure why this recipe only averages 4 stars, it deserves 5!  The texture is amazing!  So much better than any traditional cake recipe containing butter that I've tried (I know, these are different, but I much prefer this!)  The 2nd time I made a lemon version and gave it more of a layer-cake treatment, slicing it in half and adding lemon curd, whipped cream and macerated strawberry slices, then decorating the top with piped whipped cream and whole strawberries.  My new cake go-to recipe!

commentBody: Here is my Finnish grandmother's (and my Spanish mother-in law's, too!) advice so that the meringue does not start to fall in its bowl / water before use. commentBody: I had to halve the recipe because I didn't have enough eggs. I made it with 5 egg whites and 4 yolks. I followed the recipe exactly - and I have to admit it's a bit fussy but I thought it was worth it. It turned out so good! I usually make sponge cake but this cake held its shape better than a sponge and even more moist. It was like eating a pillow or a cloud. Would definitely make again. Next time I'll make the full recipe.

commentBody:
Today I made recipe exactly as written and used an old and reliable tube pan that's made many an angel food cake successfully. Cake cooked 60 mins and skewer came out dry. Nevertheless, while cooling on its bottle after 30 minutes the cake fell out. Bottom and sides had a light crust still adhering. The fallen cake was light and properly dry...not undercooked. 
Any thoughts as to why this happened?
BTW, the cake was absolutely delicious. sliced and cooled it on wire rack to preserve fluffiness

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: I made the stock in an 8 quart instant pot with the carcass of a 12 lb turkey, 2 quarts of Costco purchased stock, a little over a quart of water and big spoonful of Chicken Better than Bouillon (in other words, what I had on hand).  I cooked the carcass on high pressure for an hour and let the instant pot naturally depressurize for an hour more. The stock was delicious, and the leftover stock became a jelly the way it should.  The result with the mushrooms, eggs,  etc. was a hit.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: After making homemade (Chick,Turkey,Beef Pork, Duck) stock for 35+ years, simmering such a small amount of chick parts for 12 hours is waaaaay overkill. After a few hours, the flavor is extracted and you are wasting time and energy. While more turkey and bones are called for in the second stock, still think you'll get all the flavor and goodness out in 4 or so hours. This is from experience of going for the 12-hour simmer enough times, and then being underwhelmed with the results.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Don’t cheat and use a store bought broth; the layered cooking with raw bones then cooked ones for hours and hours makes the magic. To the final ramen broth, we added a touch of Hon Dashi for depth. We didn’t use the vinegar or Worcestershire. Instead, we made a taré that we spooned into each bowl before adding the broth to give it the flavor bomb and proper saltiness.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
I tried making this last night and the stock-making was a disaster.  There are omissions and contradictions in the recipe that I wish someone would clear up. 

1. Steps 3 & 5: Boil covered or uncovered? 

2. 6 cups or 6 quarts of chicken stock (see step 3 vs. tip)  If you boil to 6 c., you have a richer, velvety stock, but there's no way it will endure another 2 hours of high heat cooking.  I know.

The stock was gorgeous after step 3 but a charred mess after step 5. I'll try again in November.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
Tweaked a few things. Had homemade chicken stock available, made the turkey stock in an Instant Pkt using the base stock as the liquid and added turkey carcass pieces. The pressure cooker function sped up the process. That's actually how I made the base stock a few months ago. Reduced the final stock by a bit to thicken and concentrate it. Also I had no turkey leftovers so I sous/vided a turkey thigh at 165F for 2.5 hrs and then cooled it and roasted it at 450F for 7 minutes followed by a 3-min broil to crisp up the skin. Let it rest then sliced it up and assembled everything. Spectacular results and would do it again (a bunch leftover for tomorrow's dinner). 

10/10 recipe if followed exactly or make it your own.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Lots of questions about what to do with the other half of the coconut cream in the comments. I whisked the first half into the melted butter as instructed... and then whisked in the other half and kept going with the rest of the recipe as written. It worked perfectly. The bibingka came out beautifully - light, tender, delicious. If you can get your hands on the macapuno, it really makes a difference. I also sprinkled a little turbinado sugar on the top for the final bake.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
Consider wrapping your knife in a hot towel to warm it instead of running it under hot water. (Wet a towel and microwave it.) In most homes 30 seconds of kitchen faucet flow equals roughly a gallon of water (at avg flow rate of 1.8-2.2 gallons/min). Almost half the US is in drought with much of the west coast in extreme drought. That gallon of water has better uses than warming a knife.
(That said, I'll be trying this recipe this weekend, albeit with a different knife warming method!)

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: When strawberries are at their best, I sandwich halved or thickly sliced strawberries (depending on size) between buttered bread, with a sprinkle of sugar. It's like eating the freshest of strawberry jam sandwiches. The bread has to be soft, and you don't want it sliced too thick. I've also gently opened a top-sliced hot-dog roll, removed a bit of crumb, buttered the insides, sprinkling them with sugar, and filled the roll with small or halved strawberries. Easy to hold and eat!

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
Fond remembrances. When I was a (very) little girl Mommy used to make me special sandwiches for lunch:  softened cream cheese on Wonder Bread topped with cut pieces of fruit.  Eaten open-faced.

Now I often do the same for breakfast   Stir up a little ricotta until creamy, add a bit of honey, spread it on anything other than Wonder Bread. Finally arrange (not just plop on) my little cut pieces of fruit(s) in a nice design.  

Simple, "pretty" and delicious.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
These were fun to eat, but messy, in part due to the bread being the thinner-sliced Taiwanese version. Go for the Japanese bread's notably thicker slices. 

I disagree w/timing: it's far more than 20 mins to whip cream, fold it into the mascarpone and honey, cube the fruit, trim the crusts, spread the mixture onto the slices, wrap and unwrap them, and carefully slice each. Perhaps after a few times, I'd work faster, but this isn't a recipe I expect to use all that often.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Growing up on Oahu, we kept our potato salad and mac salad separate. Mac salad (my mother's recipe--forgive her, she grew up on the Mainland) included chopped hard-boiled eggs, chopped celery and green onion, tuna, a bit of sweet pickle relish, and the classic Best Foods REAL mayonnaise, but no carrots. I still make it this way 50 years later--and have been known to make a special trip to Costco for the mayonnaise (markets in Japan generally carry only Kewpie). And yes, I use an ice cream scoop.

commentBody: I don't think it's really necessary to cook the noodles "two minutes past al dente." author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Potato tip: At the 13 minute mark, add a bit of salt and perhaps a couple of teaspoons of vinegar to the boiling water. This lowers the pH of the water and slows the breakdown of the natural pectin that holds the potato together. That gives the starch time to gelatinize and stabilize the potato structure. Potatoes fall apart because the pectin "lets go" before the starch has a chance to gelatinize.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
#A.Sanchez:

American Taro is readily available at Hispanic markets. Puertorricans call it "malanga jojota" and Dominicans call it "malanga coco". Peel it, cut it, and boil it for about 20 to 40 minutes, depending on the size of the chunks. It makes a good side pureed with butter. On a pinch, substitute yautia (white or lilac).
"Real" taro can be found in Asian markets. They are bringing edoes and "regular" taro at my local Ctown (Hispanic), but I by far prefer yautia and malanga.

Here's a comment from NYT Cooking:#A.Sanchez:

American Taro is readily available at Hispanic markets. Puertorricans call it "malanga jojota" and Dominicans call it "malanga coco". Peel it, cut it, and boil it for about 20 to 40 minutes, depending on the size of the chunks. It makes a good side pureed with butter. On a pinch, substitute yautia (white or lilac).
"Real" taro can be found in Asian markets. They are bringing edoes and "regular" taro at my local Ctown (Hispanic), but I by far prefer yautia and malanga.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Macaroni is not “noodles”…at least I never heard that term used for macaroni during my 17 years in Hawai’i.  I never had macaroni salad with potatoes there, but maybe that’s because in the 1970s and ‘80s good russet potatoes were hard to come by in Hawai’i. Taro and sweet potatoes, yes, but not cooked with pasta in my experience.  The double-starch combo that I do remember from plate lunches on Kuhio Beach was “macaroni salad with two scoops rice.”  Goes good wit teriyaki beef, brah!

commentBody: Americas Test Kitchen’s Hawaiian Mac Salad advocates cooking the macaroni till they are “fat,” meaning A few minutes past al dente. A cup of sauce is reserved for a second dressing after the first dressing has time to absorb and cure.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: “Macaroni salad” = made with elbows. “Pasta salad” = made with any other kind of pasta. I’ve never seen Hawaiian macaroni salad made with diced potatoes. I’m a (basically lifelong) CT resident and my standard macaroni salad is, as it turns out, Hawaiian!  Elbows cooked past al dente, very finely chopped red onion, celery, and carrot, and chopped hard boiled egg.  And a dressing that includes oil, apple cider vinegar, paprika, sugar, celery salt, seasoned pepper - and lots of Hellman’s!

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Needs more complex flavors! I made this recipe as prescribed, and it was just OK, not great. The ancho chile was about right, but the base dough was a little bland for my taste. I made it again with these changes: Dry ingredients--increased cocoa powder to 1/2 cup, added zest of one large orange, added two tsp. cinnamon, added one tsp. coriander. I increased chocolate chips to one cup, mixing semi-sweet and dark chocolate chips. Try it!

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
Ancho gave nice, smoky flavor, but not very hot for us.  More ancho would likely be too smoky. I used sliced almonds (forgot to buy cashews!) and big chunk chocolate.  First log (20 min, soft center, as directed) was crumbly. Put 2nd log in for more bake. It was easier to slice (my oven, YMMV)  but every biscotti I've made with butter has been more crumbly than crisp. 
I plan to try ancho/chunk chocolate/cocoa (with added cayenne?), but with my 'go to' biscotti recipe that has no eggs.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
I made these today. My ancho powder is very fresh and pungent. Next time, I will use less. Perhaps mixing the Chile with the cocoa powder would help the baker get a sense of the balance. Honestly, I can’t imagine using cayenne, which packs so much more heat. 

While my batch is too hot for my morning coffee, it should work fine for cappuccino, or dessert with ice cream and fruit, or mango sorbet. The first batch is always an opportunity to learn and adjust.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: A big flop for me! I make biscotti routinely. I was surprised that this recipe had the same amount of fat and 'liquid' from the eggs despite adding two ingredients that are extremely dry, the chile powder and the cocoa. The dough would not hold together or incorporate the add ins. I think next time I will add an extra egg (a Noni would!) and try again. I have a tea cup full of chocolate dough smeared pecans and chocolate chips if anyone wants them!

commentBody:
Made these this afternoon, and they’re good.

- substituted Cup-4-Cup gluten free flour … and added a few tablespoons more to get the right consistency
- used Ancho chili powder as directed … but wanted a smokier, spicier finished product — might add some cayenne & smoky paprika next time

I cooked 20 minutes   25 minutes … would go a bit longer on the second cooking to get a crispier biscotti

Recipe made 52 biscotti

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
These were a big hit at my volunteer gig, where chiles in any form, but especially in chocolate, are revered.  My only substitution was using pasilla chile powder instead of the ancho powder.  That, and using my vintage Sunbeam hand mixer at the beginning, then switching to a very large spatula to finish mixing the stiff dough.  Easy to mix and roll.

Per reader recommendations, I used mini chocolate chips, chopped the cashews before toasting, and placed the slices on edge for the second baking.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
Did a lot of DJ mixing, and still turned out great! Tried to tone down the heat while keeping the chili flavor. Used gochugaru flakes (~1TBSP) and added a few dashes of cinnamon. Only had 1 egg, so subbed in 1.5TBSP heavy cream for the other. Almonds instead of cashews. Normal grocery cocoa powder.

A forgiving foundation recipe for chocolate biscotti, and a very exciting balance of flavors! Highly recommend even for non-spice lovers (with the obvious warning to use less chili...)

commentBody:
Be really careful with those scotch bonnets or habaneros. Pros often use gloves. If you merely touch them and forget and then rub your eyes, or guys use the bathroom, you'll remember the experience. Until the next time you forget. 
They're so hot that way I wouldn't even bother deseeding them if you're discarding them. The more you play with them the more likely the capsaicin oil gets under your fingernails or whatnot. A regular quick wash does nothing,  requires more like a scrubbing.

commentBody:

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Terrific, great recipe for W@H cook. Cut veg (extra) day before, added celery and semi-hot green peppers in addition to habaneros, since we like things spicy, skipped chayote. At noon day of, cooked veg, added parsley and 2-3 TBSP pickled golden raisins (had made to go with roast veal a week earlier).  Rather than fry fish, rubbed with spice rub and olive oil, roasted on a bed of fresh thyme @ 375 for 12 min. Poured hot veg mix on top, covered and let sit till dinner.  Served w steamed limas.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Rqaq w Adas (Lentils With Pasta) Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. The recipe reads more complicated than it is. The flavors were picked up in the noodles and the texture of the lentils and additions of pomegranate seeds and crispy onions made for a complex taste with little effort. NOTE: Garnishes definitely make the dish!  I used 9oz dry tagliatelle (at the end of step 5), store bought crispy onions & skipped frying flatbread. My natural foods store had pomegranate molasses and tamarind concentrate in a jar -not tinny. A keeper!

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Rqaq w Adas (Lentils With Pasta) Recipe
commentBody: The recipe is a bit scrambled. But if you want to skip the dough part and just use dried pasta, you can go step 1,4,5, then the second part of step 7, then steps 8&9. I used half tablespoon of salt, because I used store bought pita chips - salted, and slightly less oil than recommended.  I used 8oz dried pasta nests.  The end result was delicious, not soupy and very filling - 6 very hearty portions, more like 8 normal.  I will make double the crispy onions for the topping next time.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Rqaq w Adas (Lentils With Pasta) Recipe
commentBody: After reading Naz Deravian's Bottom of the Pot, I made a lot of substitutes. I used tamarind paste, mint instead of cilantro, cranberries instead of pomegranates, dried lemons, and sourdough fried into croutons. It was beautiful with red/green stripes, and just as delicious. I'd coat the onions in the wondra for the garnishes, and double them. I thought it might be too sour, but it all balanced out in the end.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Rqaq w Adas (Lentils With Pasta) Recipe
commentBody: Dry version: Cook lentils, strain, reserve water. Caramelize onions. Combine 1/2 lentils w all onions [cook more onion for leftover lentils]. Cook 6oz egg noodles in lentil water. Fry garlic, cilantro, & pre-fried French's onions for topping. Drizzle noodles topped w lentil+onions w combined substitute tamarind/pom molasses "sauce."/For tamarind: 1 T raspberry jam, 1/2 T balsamic vinegar/For pom molasses: 1 T Worcestershire, 1 T lemon juice, 2 t tomato paste, 2 t brown sugar, up to 2T water

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: This is an old recipe that my aunt used to make for me going back 45 to 50 years ago. The name of the dish was "Country Captain Chicken". In this version the spices used are in recipe are used in Indian dishes which I love. The original recipe called for salt, pepper, garlic and onions, oregano or thyme and a tomato puree and raisins, yes raisins.  I will try this version, but I probably will add a tablespoon or so of tomato paste. That would add a richness that goes well with the other spices.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: This recipe has nothing to do with 'The British Raj'. It is clearly a variant of a well known Malaysian dish: Chicken/Curry Kapitan and part of Nyonya food, Nyonya is a title given to married women from the Peranakan ethnic group  descended from marriages between Chinese immigrants and native Malayans, and famous for their cooking which fuses elements from both groups' culinary traditions. Originally from ports like Penang many male Peranakans were sea cooks which is why the dish has spread.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: It seems like a vindaloo dish, but without the masala and sauce (and thus flavor). Vindaloo originated in Goa, a Portuguese  colony, from a dish called “carne de vinha d’alhos,” i.e. meat with wine vinegar and garlic. It's made with many kinds of meat and seafood, with some variation in spice mixes (masalas). Thus it can get much more interesting than this recipe (and with less clean-up). If you like this recipe, explore vindaloo options too.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Uncle Glenn’s Onaga (Steamed Red Snapper With Somen) Recipe
commentBody: Stripped down, this dish can be made without the scallop powder, chungchoi or the oyster sauce as the soy sauce provides enough salt, and the fish juices will provide the rest of the flavor.  Garlic, ginger, green onions, cilantro are essential.  Fish must be fresh!  Eyes must be full and bulging, gills must be red and not slimy.  Overcooking is the ultimate crime against seafood.  10-12 minutes on high, check with chopstick.  Fish should be flaky, not hard. If cooked to bone, it’s done.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Part 2 - The souring agent for sinigang varies from region to region in the Philippines. Sour fruits, such as kamias (Averrhoa bilimbi) and batuan (Garcinia morella) are commonly used, as are kalamansi (calamondin/citrofortunella), unripe guava and pineapple. Tomatoes are almost always added. Different cuts of pork and beef, meaty fish, shrimp and other crustaceans are the commonly used proteins singly, never combined. Sometimes water used to wash rice is used to add body to the soup.

commentBody:

The first is too dirty/dusty. )  It also helps the  broth have a little more body for the sour tamarind, savory pork and umami rich fish sauce to grab onto.  

I've never heard of using powdered shiitake; I'm gonna need to try that and see what it adds to the flavor.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Try to use super ripe Manila/Ataulfo/Champagne Mangoes.  Other mango varieties can be too juicy and fibrous, resulting in an icy and stringy dessert.  It’s also best to know the true taste of the mango you are using.  These can take up to seven days to become super ripe (dark spots ok).  Each fruit should yield 1/2 cup, and you need 2 cups fruit per recipe.  Try using whatever best fruit in season to sub for mango (strawberries, peaches) or use canned, drained fruit cocktail for Crema de Fruta.

I had a bumper crop of mangoes this year (Kent) which I chopped and froze.  I thawed them out and drained some of the excess liquid for this recipe -- the freezing seems to have eliminated the stringiness and the resulting puree was quite smooth.

I used half graham crackers and half ginger snaps for the base.  For the topping in addition to the crumbs I minced some candied ginger, lime zest, and chiffonade of fresh mint (best added after removing the Supreme from the freezer) -- delicious!

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
Made this, but I used 1/4 cup sweetened condensed milk instead of 1/2 cup. The mangoes were sweet enough that it didn't need more sugar.

Used 200 grams of graham cracker crumbs, because that's all I had, and melted coconut oil instead of butter.  

Also, I used an 8" square pan lined with parchment paper. No problem getting it out of the pan, but next time I will use a 9" square pan or reduce the cream by 1/2 cup. 

Fabulous!
