author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Very good. I used a spring form pan and it filled it by more than 3/4, so if you are going to put this in a pie plate, you need to reduce the filling by half or so (or at least the whipped cream part). If I make again, I will increase the mango by 50% and do three layers to increase the mango flavor. Did add lime to the mango and liked the result. I froze layer by layer as suggested by others and that worked well, no need to freeze for 8 hours that way.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
I made this with Trader Joe's speculoos cookies, it was terrific!  Next time, I'll cut back on the cream and use a little less condensed milk because I have about half the mixture left over, and it still was mostly cream and not enough fruit flavor (2 cups).  I might try this with apricots and mango because the mango, though beautifully ripe, was not flavorful enough to assert itself over the sweetness of the cream.  
Still, good enough for a re-do -- yum!

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Despite expecting failure, this worked beautifully made as-is! Used 5 Kent mangoes (I live in Ontario, not mango country, also not peach season here). I cut off the flesh (avoiding fibrous inner parts), kept sealed in the fridge overnight and drained off excess liquid in morning. Cream was beautiful. Pulp not icy/stringy. Served it maybe too thawed; could have been firmer. But it still cut okay and crust didn't completely fall apart. CRUST AMAZING but browned butter lost some depth when frozen.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: I've made this a few times and it's always a hit at family parties. I stay pretty faithful to the recipe, other than adding a pinch of salt to the crust and the whipped cream (I like salty sweets)! Since the recipe is fairly simple and lets the mangoes shine, I think it's important to only use atafulo/honey mangoes that are really ripe and in season for the best results.

I did find I needed to let the pie sit at room temperature for at least an hour after being in the freezer, so that the mango layer could soften up some.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: My family always whole graham crackers, not pulsed. And because all purpose cream is the more ubiquitous cream found in Philippine stores, we use that combined with condensed milk. The result is quite liquidy. But that’s okay because it will seep through to the crackers making them soft. Also we cut the mangoes in thin flat slices and layer them close so there are no spaces in between. We top it with the cream and condensed milk mixture. Overnight in the fridge and ready to serve the next day.

commentBody:

The recipe says to use 2 cups of the mango purée, I used 1 cup per layer on a 9in springform and 1.5 cups would have made a better layer.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
This is a very forgiving recipe. I had to make two 8 inch pies. I used 2 1/2 cups of heavy cream, 6 oz of sweetened condensed milk, and 2 1/2 cups of mango puree. I added cinnamon to the graham cracker crust and vanilla to the cream filling. Amounts were perfect and the pies just disappeared. Delicious!
When ripe mangos are not available, all South Asian grocery stores carry Kesar, a large can of mango pulp. I think that size can would make 3 pies. Or make ice cream with the leftover pulp!

recipeTitle: Oxtail Stew in Peanut Sauce (Kare-Kare) Recipe
commentBody:
I first cooked a variation of this with a recipe from a Filipino friend, who said she was influenced by a Vietnamese cook.  Anyway, don't substitute for the oxtails, which exude much more collagen than short ribs.  Makes a big difference in taste.  Also substituted turmeric and smoked paprika
Secondly, I use Adams (or any other) real peanut butter that hasn't had the peanut oil extracted.  Can't imagine why one would introduce added sugar and cheap vegetable oil found in Jiff.

author: Ligaya Mishan
recipeTitle: Oxtail Stew in Peanut Sauce (Kare-Kare) Recipe
commentBody: This certainly veers from the traditional kare-kare, such that I would call it kare-kare inspired, but not the dish itself. The traditional kare-kare is unadorned by alcohol, soy, celery, carrots, and thyme (!). My mother would typically use fresh ground peanut butter without added sugar. Achiote oil is used to give it a saffron orange hue. The dish is intentionally left under salted, to make allowances for a small amount of briny bagoong (salted shrimp paste) that is served on the side.

recipeTitle: Oxtail Stew in Peanut Sauce (Kare-Kare) Recipe
commentBody: Bagoong comes in different varieties, depending on what is used as the base. Bagoong monamon is fermented anchovies. Mine is Bagong Balayan, which is Bagoong monamon made in Balayan. bagoong terong is made with bonnet fish. See wikipedia for more. Now that I have some, and read about it, you could use fish sauce as a substitute. Seems like the only real difference is the fish sauce is skimmed off the top, and bagoong is the solids. And for meat? Cow neck- purchased from a Spanish butcher.

commentBody:
I always study the recipe and comments here prior to doing a first run. I've yet to try this recipe. As I read thru this recipe, I kept stumbling over the suggested times because none of the ingredients and their cook times line up. Prep the carrots, and if you follow the suggested time the carrots will be hard and mostly uncooked. I hate raw carrots, no teeth. Ox Tails take up to 6 hours to make them edible. The green beans? The Bok Choy?

Someone please reconsider the length of times.

commentBody: As a German family living in this country, this is exactly the item we have been adding to our otherwise very traditional American Thanksgiving meal as the token German side dish for many years! As I also prepare it from scratch, I recommend to marinate the sliced red cabbage the night before it is cooked. Marinade should include vinegar, red wine (or apple juice), sugar (or honey), and cloves. And I agree, it needs to cook more than 20 minutes to become as soft as it should be.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: Have made a very similar recipe to this for more than 30 years.  The difference between this recipe and the one I use is that mine contains three sliced or chopped onions, no cornstarch & no cayenne but it does contain ½ teaspoon ground cinnamon and ½ teaspoon dried thyme.  The cooking method is also different - all ingredients are divided into two layers in a covered ovenproof dish and cooked in the oven at 180°C  (355°) for 2 ½ hours. Really excellent winter dish.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody: This is my favorite part of the Thanksgiving meal having grown up/ lived in Munich, Germany for 10 years. Its a great addition to the bland (in my opinion) turkey fare. I have adjusted my recipe over the years to include sweetening it with honey. Using butter and oil and different spices and levels of heat. Either way its deliciously sweet and sour and a holiday favorite. I love surprising people with how delicious Germanic food is when done right.

author: Ligaya Mishan
commentBody:
This turkey surpassed all expectations. I used a few ground spices instead of the whole ones, using a ground-to-whole conversion chart. We had a free range, 14.2 pound turkey. It was very lean and rendered very little fat and liquid for basting. And it reached temperature about a half hour early. Next time I might throw some dried habanero flakes into the rub for its fruitiness and heat.

Tonight we're making a risotto from the stock and the leftover turkey. Can't wait!

commentBody: I used this a guide for egg rolls. I started with ground pork and because it didn’t have any cabbage, I substituted shredded carrots and shredded fennel. It was an excellent combination and even my fussy teen son liked it.
